projects.title: A value-driven framework to model the impact of commercial thinning on timber supply and climate change mitigation potential in BC's interior forests
projects.summary: A completed commercial thinning and diverse-planting study on adapting to climate-change-induced timber supply shortage in British Columbia.
body: This project evaluated silvicultural strategies for adapting to climate-change-induced timber supply shortage in British Columbia.

The published study examined how thinning and diverse planting scenarios can affect future timber supply under climate change, connecting treatment choices, stand development, and landscape-scale supply outcomes.

The main project is now closed and documented through the Canadian Journal of Forest Research paper, Combining thinning and diverse plantings to adapt to climate-change-induced timber supply shortage in British Columbia. FRESH remains interested in follow-up projects that build on this work, especially where thinning, climate adaptation, carbon outcomes, or operational feasibility can be studied with updated models and data.
outputs: Canadian Journal of Forest Research paper
Follow-up project ideas and expressions of interest are welcome

references.citation: Lafond, V., Polinko, A. D., Man, C. D., Dymond, C. C., Paradis, G. E., and Griess, V. C. 2024. Combining thinning and diverse plantings to adapt to climate-change-induced timber supply shortage in British Columbia. Canadian Journal of Forest Research, 54(11), 1230-1253.

projects.title: Yunhao (Davis) Xu MASc thesis
projects.summary: An active MASc thesis project developing a principal-agent, rolling-horizon optimization model for post-wildfire salvage harvesting and policy design in British Columbia.
body: Yunhao (Davis) Xu's MASc thesis, working-titled Modeling Salvage Harvesting After Wildfires: A Linear Bi-level Principal-agent Approach, develops decision-support models for post-fire salvage harvesting in British Columbia.

The project frames salvage harvesting as a principal-agent problem: the forest landowner or policy-maker wants to recover value, maintain timber supply, and support forest recovery, while forest companies decide whether salvage operations are economically feasible.

The modelling workflow combines rolling-horizon planning with single-level and bi-level optimization so the system can update as wildfire disturbance, burn severity, timber degradation, and salvage availability change through time.

A Williams Lake Timber Supply Area case study uses controlled semi-synthetic inputs built from public forest inventory, timber-supply-area, fire burn severity, and elevation datasets to test salvage schedules and policy instruments.

The thesis evaluates instruments such as stumpage credits, salvage windows, volume constraints, and harvest-sequence rules, with outputs focused on discounted net present value, product-grade mix, salvage volume over time, retention, regeneration, subsidy allocation, and stand-selection patterns.

The planned public research contribution is an open-source Python modelling framework with preprocessing scripts, optimization model files, scenario data, solver configuration, and documentation for reproducible wildfire-salvage planning experiments.
outputs: Principal-agent optimization model for post-wildfire salvage harvesting
Williams Lake Timber Supply Area case-study model inputs and scenarios
Planned open-source Python modelling framework, documentation, and reproducible scenario materials
Thesis, code, and publication links when publicly available

references.citation: Xu, Y. Modeling Salvage Harvesting After Wildfires: A Linear Bi-level Principal-agent Approach. MASc thesis in progress, University of British Columbia.

projects.title: Jamie Iversen MASc thesis
projects.summary: An active MASc thesis project developing a fuel-treatment planning decision-support tool for optimizing wildfire-risk-reduction treatments in British Columbia.
body: Jamie Iversen's MASc thesis, working-titled A Fuel Treatment Planning Decision Support Tool, develops modelling workflows for landscape-level wildfire fuel-treatment planning in British Columbia.

The project responds to the difficulty of choosing where, when, and how intensively to apply fuel treatments across large forest landscapes when managers must consider wildfire risk, treatment cost, forest structure, and budget constraints over long time horizons.

The first thesis component is a BC fuel-treatment decision support guide. The guide is designed as a flowchart that characterizes stands by fire weather zone, biogeoclimatic zone, leading species, stand structure, fuel conditions, and management goals before assigning treatment logic for modelling use.

The second component applies that treatment logic in a spatially explicit forest estate model using Patchworks. The model is intended to optimize the timing, placement, and intensity of fuel treatments at the landscape level under budgetary constraints.

The case study focuses on the 100 Mile House Timber Supply Area, a south-central British Columbia landscape with wildfire exposure, multiple communities, important evacuation corridors, and forest conditions where fuel-treatment planning is directly relevant.

The planned modelling workflow uses VRI-derived forest attributes, analysis-unit treatment curves, and wildfire-behaviour or human-fire-interaction metrics such as suppression effectiveness, suppression cost, expected damages, crown-fire potential, rate of spread, intensity, and severity.
outputs: MASc thesis proposal and thesis materials
BC fuel-treatment decision support guide for modelling use
Fuel-treatment curves for analysis units in the 100 Mile House Timber Supply Area
Scenario outputs for treatment timing, placement, intensity, and budget-constrained tradeoffs
Thesis, code, and publication links when publicly available

references.citation: Iversen, J. A Fuel Treatment Planning Decision Support Tool. MASc thesis proposal, University of British Columbia.

projects.title: CCCANDiES
projects.summary: Open cumulative-effects modelling for climate change, anthropogenic disturbance, natural disturbance, forest ecosystem services, carbon, and forest-sector decision support.
body: CCCANDiES stands for Cumulative effects of Climate Change, Anthropogenic and Natural Disturbances on forest Ecosystem Services.

The project develops open modelling infrastructure for forecasting how managed forest landscapes respond to climate change, wildfire and other natural disturbances, timber harvesting, road networks, and forest-sector supply-chain decisions.

A central technical goal is to connect ecosystem-process simulation tools such as SpaDES, LandR, fire and carbon modules with forest estate and wood-supply optimization tools such as ws3. This makes it possible to represent both stochastic ecosystem processes and explicit management decision processes in the same reproducible workflow.

The research program includes open software modules for spatially explicit harvest and road scheduling, forest-sector supply-chain modelling, carbon stock and flux accounting, rolling-horizon re-planning, and agent-based or game-theoretic representations of policy and industry interactions.

The project also develops multi-objective tradeoff methods for generating many forest-management scenarios, estimating high-dimensional Pareto tradeoff surfaces, projecting those surfaces into interpretable decision spaces, and selecting representative scenario sets for participatory planning and multi-criteria decision analysis.

Applied case-study work includes cumulative-effects scenarios for natural disturbance and forest harvesting impacts on forest carbon, with emphasis on transparent, open, defensible, interoperable, and reusable workflows that can be inspected and extended by researchers, governments, practitioners, and partners.

The broader lab infrastructure plan behind CCCANDiES combines cloud computing, technical workstations, open datasets, version-controlled code, and knowledge-transfer workflows so that complex forest ecosystem and forest-sector models can move from proof-of-concept research prototypes toward usable open innovation tools.
people: Gregory Paradis: Principal Investigator and FRESH lead
outputs: SpaDES and ws3 interoperability modules and examples
Cumulative-effects scenarios for natural disturbance, forest harvesting, and forest carbon
Open software modules for spatial harvest scheduling, road scheduling, forest-sector supply chains, carbon accounting, and rolling-horizon workflows
Multi-objective scenario generation and tradeoff-surface methods for participatory forest planning
Reusable datasets, workflow templates, reports, manuscripts, and trainee outputs as they become public
partners: Natural Resources Canada / Canadian Forest Service collaboration context
Digital Research Alliance of Canada cloud-computing infrastructure context
FRESH lab open innovation collaborators and partners

references.citation: Paradis, G. ws3: a Wood Supply Simulation System (Version 1.0.0-alpha) [Computer software]. Zenodo, 2021.

projects.title: NSERC Discovery Grant research program
projects.summary: The FRESH NSERC Discovery Grant base-funding program for integrated sustainable forest management, forest-sector systems modelling, ecosystem-service decision support, open research software, and trainee projects.
body: NSERC Discovery Grants provide base funding for a PI research program. In FRESH, this grant supports the lab platform behind many connected modelling, software, thesis, and partnership projects rather than a single stand-alone study.
The FRESH Discovery Grant research program develops open, scalable, and reusable modelling tools for integrated sustainable forest management and forest-sector supply-chain capacity planning.

The program focuses on systems-analysis methods that link forest ecosystem planning, industrial fibre procurement, processing-capacity decisions, logistics, carbon, and ecosystem-service outcomes in a common decision-support framework.

Core methodological work includes spatially explicit harvest and road-construction optimization, forest-sector supply-chain optimization, soft- and hard-linked model formulations, hybrid simulation-optimization, carbon modelling, tradeoff analysis, and open research software built around ws3 and related tools.

The related-project links below are intentionally broad: each listed project has received some direct or indirect support from the Discovery Grant base-funding platform, such as trainee time, software infrastructure, modelling methods, reusable data workflows, supervision, or research-program continuity. Other partner grants and contracts may also be primary funding sources for those projects.
outputs: Base funding for related FRESH modelling, software, and thesis projects
Open research software and reusable modelling infrastructure
Trainee projects and student research outputs
Program publications, reports, repositories, and public research outputs

related_projects.label: Mitacs, Newmont, and forestry decarbonization modelling

projects.title: FlashForest drone seeding microsite machine learning
projects.status: Ongoing applied research and software project
projects.summary: Applied machine-learning work with FlashForest on classifying drone-imagery microsites for aerial seeding and reforestation decision support.
body: This project develops a reproducible machine-learning pipeline for classifying reforestation microsites from high-resolution drone imagery, supporting drone-based tree planting and seed-deployment decisions after disturbance.

The current workflow treats microsite selection as a four-class semantic segmentation problem: background, good, fair, and poor. RGB orthomosaic tiles are matched to pixel-wise segmentation masks prepared in Roboflow by forestry experts.

A key technical challenge is severe class imbalance: most labelled pixels belong to the poor class, while good and fair microsites are much rarer. The project therefore emphasizes spatial data splitting, per-class intersection-over-union metrics, and careful interpretation of aggregate accuracy.

The baseline model uses a U-Net encoder-decoder architecture on 512 by 512 RGB image tiles. Experiments have tested early stopping, geometric augmentation, photometric augmentation, weighted sparse categorical cross-entropy, Dice loss, filtering low-information tiles, and oversampling informative good and fair tiles.

The public ML4seeding repository contains notebooks for orthomosaic metadata review, pseudo-orthomosaic construction for A10 and A58 image segments, image tiling, grid overlays for annotation, U-Net training, predicted-mask stitching, drone flight-path preparation, and operations-research post-processing concepts.

The current finding is that the training pipeline is usable as a baseline, but future performance gains will depend heavily on data quality, annotation consistency, and collecting more examples of the minority good and fair microsite classes.

The longer-term decision-support goal is to turn georeferenced prediction layers into operational planning inputs, including candidate planting points, spatial thinning, and drone path or mission-planning models.
people: Elaheh Ghasemi: past postdoctoral researcher and machine-learning pipeline lead
Salar Ghotb: past postdoctoral researcher and project contributor
Gregory Paradis: FRESH lead and supervisor
outputs: Orthomosaic review, pseudo-orthomosaic construction, and image-tiling workflows
Evaluation workflow using accuracy, mean IoU, foreground mean IoU, and per-class IoU
Predicted segmentation-mask and georeferenced output workflow prototypes
Drone flight-path and operations-research post-processing prototypes

projects.title: CLEWs-C2070 NRCan grant
projects.summary: A collaborative NRCan project rebuilding forest and biomass supply modelling for CLEWs-C2070 using open ws3 and fresh_fibre components.
body: CLEWs-C2070 focuses on innovative integration of climate, land, and water into energy models for addressing Canada's net-zero commitments.
FRESH is rebuilding the forestry and biomass supply component from transferred Forest Fibre Cascade Model archive evidence into an open, maintainable modelling path.

The current diagnostic architecture links ws3-shaped forest harvest outputs to fresh_fibre, a downstream profit-maximizing network-flow linear programming model for fibre allocation, processing capacity, residues, markets, and exports.

Recent prototype work has established a BC mini-network, typed CSV contracts, PuLP/CBC solve path, ws3 export bridge, SCANFI diagnostic inventory fixture, and Gauthier et al. 2015 yield-policy evaluation path. This work remains diagnostic rather than production-calibrated.
partners: Hisham Zerriffi: UBC Department of Forest Resources Management
outputs: FFCM archive reverse-engineering notes and implementation-boundary matrices
BC mini-network, ws3-smoke, and SCANFI-diagnostic supply variants

projects.title: Single-stem snap-and-fly helicopter logging productivity
projects.summary: A Forest Action Lab collaboration studying productivity, cycle-time structure, and operational drivers of single-stem snap-and-fly helicopter logging in coastal British Columbia.
body: This project studies standing-stem snap-and-fly helicopter logging as a specialized forest operations system for selectively extracting high-value stems.

The work quantifies productivity, cycle-time structure, delay occurrence, payload characteristics, and operational drivers using field observations, video analysis, GNSS flight tracking, mapped stem locations, and contractor-provided payload data.

The case study focuses on coastal British Columbia operations using a Kaman K-Max K-1200 and a Sikorsky S-61N, with FRESH contributing modelling, analysis, and research-record support in collaboration with the UBC Forest Action Lab.
people: Omar Mologni: Forest Action Lab paper lead
Dominik Roeser: Forest Action Lab collaborator
partners: UBC Department of Forest Resources Management
outputs: Field observation, video-analysis, GNSS flight-tracking, mapped-stem, and payload datasets
Manuscript and publication link when publicly available

projects.title: GNSS-based area-cover validation for whole-tree logging equipment
projects.summary: A Forest Action Lab collaboration evaluating GNSS-based methods for estimating daily area covered by ground-based whole-tree logging equipment.
body: This project evaluates how GNSS recording frequency and post-processing choices affect estimates of daily area covered by ground-based whole-tree logging equipment.

The analysis compares machine-track-based estimates against field-derived validation data from multiple British Columbia logging operations, with attention to spatial error, operational scale, and the cost of collecting high-frequency GNSS data.
The work supports better digital monitoring of forest operations by connecting machine positioning data, UAV photogrammetry, stand and site information, and productivity analysis.
people: Omar Mologni: Forest Action Lab paper lead
Dominik Roeser: Forest Action Lab collaborator
partners: UBC Department of Forest Resources Management
outputs: GNSS machine-track and UAV photogrammetry validation workflow
Manuscript and publication link when publicly available

projects.title: Hemlock dwarf mistletoe spread modelling
projects.summary: A collaborative modelling project developing manuscript outputs on hemlock dwarf mistletoe spread and infection dynamics.
body: This project focuses on modelling hemlock dwarf mistletoe spread and infection dynamics in British Columbia forests.
The work connects forest health, spatial spread modelling, variable-retention harvesting, and decision-support questions around hemlock dwarf mistletoe.
Two thesis-derived manuscripts are in development: one on forest-edge spread and implications for variable-retention harvesting, and one on mid-rotation hemlock dwarf mistletoe infection.
outputs: Mistletoe spread modelling workflows and collaborator notes

projects.title: WS3
projects.summary: An open-source Python framework for integrated simulation and optimization of forest landscape and wood supply systems.
body: WS3 is the Wood Supply Simulation System, an open-source Python framework for integrated simulation and optimization of forest landscape and wood supply systems.

The framework supports transparent, reproducible forest estate modelling and decision-support workflows, including links to landscape simulation, wood supply analysis, optimization, carbon modelling, and related research applications.
The public project record connects the software repository, technical documentation, citation metadata, examples, and publications as the FRESH software portfolio is curated.
people: Gregory Paradis: maintainer or lead developer
outputs: Example model instances and related decision-support applications

projects.title: FHOPS
projects.summary: Forest Harvesting Operations Planning System software for reproducible forest operations planning experiments.
body: FHOPS is the Forest Harvesting Operations Planning System, an open software project in the FRESH research software portfolio.
The project supports reproducible optimization and benchmarking workflows for forest harvesting operations planning.

FHOPS is connected to Rosalia Jaffray's MASc thesis: the literature-review chapter is published in the International Journal of Forest Engineering, the FHOPS software paper has been submitted to SoftwareX, and the rolling-horizon machine-scheduling chapter is being rewritten for submission to the Canadian Journal of Forest Research.
people: Gregory Paradis: maintainer or lead developer
outputs: Published systematic review of forest harvesting operational planning tools
Submitted SoftwareX manuscript on the FHOPS optimization and benchmarking stack
Rolling-horizon machine-scheduling manuscript being rewritten for CJFR submission

references.citation: Jaffray, R., Coupland, K., and Paradis, G. 2026. Forest harvesting operational planning tools: a systematic review of optimization, simulation, and spatial decision support systems. International Journal of Forest Engineering.

projects.title: FEMIC
projects.summary: A FRESH software stack for compiling forest inventory and yield-curve data for computational experiments.
body: FEMIC is an open software project for compiling inventory and yield-curve data for forest modelling computational experiments.
The project sits inside the FRESH open modelling stack and supports reproducible model-instance preparation for forest estate and cumulative-effects analysis.
The public project record connects the FEMIC repository, technical documentation, software citation, and related public model-instance documentation.
people: Gregory Paradis: maintainer or lead developer

projects.title: Nemora
projects.summary: A Python package for fitting probability density functions to tree stem tally data from forest inventory plots.
body: Nemora streamlines fitting statistical probability density functions to tree stem tally data from forest inventory plots.
The package supports FRESH work on modelling tree diameter distributions, including diameter-distribution fitting papers and related forest inventory analysis workflows.
The public project record links the software repository, technical documentation, citation metadata, and related publications as they are curated.
people: Gregory Paradis: maintainer or lead developer

projects.title: Modelwright
projects.summary: Software for extracting, validating, and reforging spreadsheet models as pure Python code.
body: Modelwright extracts, validates, and reforges spreadsheet models as pure Python code.
The project supports FRESH work on transparent, testable modelling workflows when policy, planning, or calculator-style models start as spreadsheets.
The public project record links the repository and documentation site while examples and related applications are curated.
people: Gregory Paradis: maintainer or lead developer
outputs: Examples and related model conversion workflows

projects.title: FABLE-Pyculator
projects.summary: Notebook controls for working with Modelwright-generated Python versions of FABLE Calculator models.
body: FABLE-Pyculator provides FABLE Calculator-specific notebook controls for Modelwright-generated Python models.
The project connects FRESH software work on spreadsheet-model transformation with land-system and food-system calculator workflows.
The public project record will connect the repository, FABLE white paper context, examples, and any related documentation as the software matures.
people: Gregory Paradis: maintainer or lead developer
outputs: FABLE white paper and related land-system modelling context

projects.title: figrecover
projects.summary: Public-alpha Python software for recovering approximate source tables from published figures when original data were not released.
body: figrecover is public-alpha Python software for recovering approximate tabular data from scientific and professional figures when the original source tables were not released.

The workflow combines deterministic PDF and manifest preparation, optional local vision-language-model metadata proposals, calibrated pixel-to-data extraction, auditable CSV/JSON outputs, and review artifacts such as overlays, metrics, and accepted-table manifests.

FRESH uses figrecover for technical-document workflows where figure-derived data need to be traceable before they become modelling inputs, including FEMIC, FHOPS, and other forest-sector modelling pipelines.
people: Gregory Paradis: maintainer or lead developer
outputs: Integration adapters for FEMIC, FHOPS, and generic modelling workflows

projects.title: fresh-hectaresbc
projects.summary: A FRESH project recovering, inventorying, documenting, and modernizing the archived HectaresBC geospatial data collection.
body: fresh-hectaresbc preserves and modernizes the archived HectaresBC geospatial data collection for British Columbia.
The project rebuilds access around reproducible catalog metadata, a DataLad/git-annex data repository, a Python API, a command-line interface, and a static browser catalog with map-preview routes.

The recovered payload set is managed outside the code repository in the UBC-FRESH/fresh-hectaresbc-data data repository, while compact catalog metadata, documentation, API tools, and browser-catalog code live in the main software repository.
people: Gregory Paradis: maintainer or lead developer
outputs: Static browser catalog and map-preview workflows

projects.title: badc
projects.summary: A FRESH open software project with public documentation and software citation metadata.
body: badc is part of the FRESH open software portfolio.
The project has public documentation and software citation metadata, and will be described in more detail as the related modelling workflow is curated.
The public project record links the repository, documentation, and citation record so the software output is discoverable from the lab website.
people: Gregory Paradis: maintainer or lead developer

projects.title: Stem diameter distribution fitting
projects.summary: A pair of reproducible stem diameter distribution fitting manuscripts connected to Forest Science, Nemora, and FRESH forest biometrics workflows.
body: This project tracks two related stem diameter distribution fitting manuscripts and their reproducible analysis workflows.

The published Forest Science paper, A Two-Stage Fitting Method for Truncated Stem Diameter Distributions, introduces a two-stage workflow that allows analysts to recover truncated Weibull and gamma solutions while retaining familiar complete-form density implementations.

The companion horizontal point sampling manuscript, A Weighted Fitting Approach for Diameter Distributions from Horizontal Point Sampling, develops a weighted fitting approach for diameter distributions from size-biased HPS tallies, with reproducible scripts and manuscript sources available in the public repository.
The work is connected to Nemora and broader FRESH efforts to make forest inventory, distribution fitting, and model-input preparation more transparent and reproducible.

references.citation: Paradis, G. E. 2026. A Two-Stage Fitting Method for Truncated Stem Diameter Distributions. Forest Science.
Paradis, G. E. A Weighted Fitting Approach for Diameter Distributions from Horizontal Point Sampling. Manuscript and reproducible workflow.

projects.title: roads R package and paper
projects.summary: An R package and Landscape Ecology paper on automated forest road projection methods for forest-change and wildlife-relevant modelling workflows.
body: The roads R package provides iterative least-cost-path and minimum-spanning-tree methods for projecting forest road networks.

The package connects target locations to existing road networks using user-supplied cost rasters and weighting functions, including options that penalize steep grades. The methods are intended for integration into R workflows and forest-change modelling frameworks over multiple time steps.

The related Landscape Ecology paper develops and assesses automated forest road projection methods using performance metrics relevant for wildlife, with public paper, software, and data links available for reuse.

references.citation: Hughes, J., Endicott, S., Lapins, D., Lochhead, K., and Paradis, G. 2025. Development and assessment of automated forest road projection methods using performance metrics relevant for wildlife. Landscape Ecology, 40, 208.

projects.title: Jinming (Jimmy) Ke MSc thesis
projects.summary: A completed MSc thesis on system-level climate impact assessment for forest harvesting, fertilization, and harvested wood product use scenarios.
body: Jinming (Jimmy) Ke's MSc thesis developed an open-source forest system climate impact assessment model for evaluating how forest harvesting, fertilization, and harvested wood product use strategies affect climate outcomes.

The thesis connects forest carbon sequestration, forest ecosystem carbon pool dynamics, harvested wood product life-cycle emissions, and avoided emissions from product substitution into a system-level assessment framework.

Scenario analysis explored different harvesting and fertilization choices and highlighted how end-use assumptions for harvested wood products, including long-lived products such as cross-laminated timber and bioenergy substitution pathways, can change the apparent climate performance of forest management strategies.
The project is part of the FRESH research thread on transparent forest-sector climate mitigation modelling and provides a public thesis foundation for subsequent manuscript and software work.
outputs: Open-source forest system climate impact assessment modelling workflow
Scenario analysis for harvesting, fertilization, and harvested wood product use
Thesis-derived manuscript and software links can be added when public

references.citation: Ke, J. 2024. Climate impact assessment under different forest harvesting and fertilization scenarios. MSc thesis, University of British Columbia.

projects.title: Yancun (Walter) Yan MSc thesis
projects.summary: A completed MSc thesis on integrated optimization of forest-sector climate change mitigation potential in British Columbia.
body: Yancun (Walter) Yan's MSc thesis developed an integrated mathematical modelling framework to simulate and optimize climate change mitigation potential in British Columbia's forest sector.

The framework combines a Strategic Forest Management Model, Forest Carbon Budget Model, and Wood Product Carbon Model to track indicators including forest carbon stocks and net carbon emissions from the forest sector.

Using the Prince George Timber Supply Area as a case study, the thesis compared scenarios ranging from no harvest to maximum harvest, tested the linkage between strategic forest management and carbon-budget modelling, and evaluated tradeoffs between timber supply and forest carbon outcomes.

The optimization work used epsilon-constraint methods to generate Pareto front curves for competing objectives such as maximizing wood supply, maximizing total forest carbon stock, and minimizing net forest carbon emissions, with sensitivity analysis around harvested wood product longevity and substitution effects.
A thesis-derived journal manuscript is in development from this work.
outputs: Pareto-front tradeoff analysis for timber supply and forest carbon outcomes

references.citation: Yan, Y. 2025. A mathematical modeling framework to optimize the climate change mitigation potential of the forest sector in British Columbia, Canada. MSc thesis, University of British Columbia.

projects.title: Rosalia Jaffray MASc thesis
projects.summary: A completed MASc thesis developing and evaluating FHOPS, an open-source machine-scheduling tool for forest operational planning.
body: Rosalia Jaffray's MASc thesis developed and evaluated FHOPS, the Forest Harvesting Operations Planning System, as an open-source tool for machine scheduling in forest operational planning.
The first thesis chapter, a systematic review of forest harvesting operational planning tools, has been published in the International Journal of Forest Engineering.
The second chapter, the FHOPS software paper, has been submitted to SoftwareX.
The third chapter evaluates rolling-horizon design for forest operations machine scheduling and is being rewritten for submission to the Canadian Journal of Forest Research.
outputs: Published systematic review of forest harvesting operational planning tools
FHOPS open-source software and benchmarking workflows
Submitted SoftwareX manuscript on the FHOPS optimization and benchmarking stack
Rolling-horizon machine-scheduling manuscript being rewritten for CJFR submission

references.citation: Jaffray, R. 2026. Development and evaluation of an open-source tool for machine scheduling in forest operational planning. MASc thesis, University of British Columbia.

Jaffray, R., Coupland, K., and Paradis, G. 2026. Forest harvesting operational planning tools: a systematic review of optimization, simulation, and spatial decision support systems. International Journal of Forest Engineering.

projects.title: Mitacs, Newmont, and forestry decarbonization modelling
projects.summary: A Mitacs Accelerate industrial postdoc project with Newmont on forest-management scenarios as nature-based decarbonization options for the mining sector.
body: This project explored how forest management and forest-sector modelling can inform nature-based decarbonization options for mining-sector decision makers.
The work connected mining-sector net-zero planning, voluntary carbon-market quality concerns, forest carbon modelling, and ecosystem-service indicators in a prototype decision-support framework.

The project was structured as a Mitacs Accelerate Industrial Postdoc internship with Newmont as the industry partner. FRESH contributed forest-sector modelling, scenario design, and interpretation of forestry-based climate and ecosystem-service outcomes.

The research compared forest-management scenarios through indicators including carbon stock, net emissions, old-growth area, species diversity, revenue, and jobs. The intent was decision support: helping decision makers understand tradeoffs among forest management, climate mitigation, biodiversity, and economic outcomes rather than presenting a deployed operational planning system.

A follow-on manuscript is in preparation from the final project report under the working title Developing a decision support system to assess forest management scenarios as a nature-based decarbonization solution: A case study from a mining company in British Columbia, Canada.
people: Elaheh Ghasemi: postdoctoral researcher and manuscript lead
Gregory Paradis: supervisor and FRESH lead
outputs: Prototype decision-support framework for comparing forest management scenarios
Forest carbon and ecosystem-service indicator modelling workflows
Scenario outputs for carbon stock, net emissions, old-growth area, species diversity, revenue, and jobs
Final technical report for project partners
Journal manuscript in preparation based on the project report

references.citation: Ghasemi, E., Ghotb, S., Coupland, K., and Paradis, G. Developing a decision support system to assess forest management scenarios as a nature-based decarbonization solution: A case study from a mining company in British Columbia, Canada. Manuscript in preparation.
